content: Data classification has 4 standard tiers: Public (marketing materials, open APIs), Internal (employee directories, policies), Confidential (customer data, financials, source code), and Restricted (PII, PHI, payment cards, trade secrets). Each tier requires escalating security controls — encryption is mandatory for Confidential and above.

Personally Identifiable Information (PII) includes direct identifiers (full name, SSN, passport number, email address, phone number, biometric data) and indirect identifiers (date of birth, ZIP code, IP address, device ID). Combining just 3 indirect identifiers (gender, birth date, ZIP code) can uniquely identify 87% of the US population.

Data minimization principle (GDPR Art. 5(1)(c)): Collect only data that is adequate, relevant, and limited to what is necessary for the stated purpose. Audit data collection forms — remove fields not essential to the service. Apply the 'need to know' principle: employees should access only data required for their role.

AES-256 encryption at rest is the gold standard for protecting stored data. It uses a 256-bit key with 2^256 possible combinations (practically unbreakable by brute force). AWS uses AES-256 for S3 server-side encryption (SSE-S3, SSE-KMS, SSE-C). Key management: rotate keys every 90-365 days, never store keys alongside encrypted data.

TLS 1.3 encrypts data in transit with 0-RTT resumption (vs 2-RTT in TLS 1.2), reducing latency by 100ms on resumed connections. TLS 1.3 removed vulnerable algorithms (RC4, SHA-1, RSA key exchange). Enforce HSTS (HTTP Strict Transport Security) with max-age=31536000 (1 year) and includeSubDomains to prevent downgrade attacks.

Data retention policy framework: Define retention periods per data category (transaction records 7 years for tax, access logs 90 days, marketing consent 3 years from last interaction). Automate deletion with scheduled jobs. Document the legal basis for each retention period. GDPR requires demonstrating that data is not kept longer than necessary.

GDPR Right to Erasure (Article 17) requires data controllers to delete personal data without undue delay when: consent is withdrawn, data is no longer necessary, individual objects to processing, or data was unlawfully processed. Exceptions include legal obligations and public health. Respond to erasure requests within 30 days (extendable to 90 for complex requests).

Right to be forgotten implementation: Identify all systems storing the user's data (databases, backups, caches, logs, third-party processors). Delete or anonymize across all systems. For backups, either overwrite or flag for deletion at next rotation. Document the deletion process and retain a minimal record (hashed ID + deletion date) to prevent re-collection.

GDPR data breach notification: Report to supervisory authority within 72 hours of becoming aware (Article 33). Notify affected individuals 'without undue delay' if high risk (Article 34). Report must include: nature of breach, categories/number of data subjects, likely consequences, and mitigation measures. Average GDPR fine for late notification is 2-4% of annual turnover.

US data breach notification varies by state: California requires notification within 72 hours for healthcare data, most states require 'reasonable' timeframe (30-90 days). HIPAA requires 60-day notification. SEC requires disclosure within 4 business days for public companies. Federal agencies must report to CISA within 72 hours per CIRCIA (2024).

Privacy by Design 7 foundational principles (Ann Cavoukian): 1) Proactive not reactive, 2) Privacy as default setting, 3) Privacy embedded into design, 4) Full functionality (positive-sum, not zero-sum), 5) End-to-end lifecycle protection, 6) Visibility and transparency, 7) Respect for user privacy. GDPR Article 25 codifies these principles as legal requirements.

Privacy by Design implementation: Conduct privacy impact assessment during design phase (not after). Default to opt-in for data collection. Implement data minimization in database schema (don't add 'nice to have' fields). Build deletion/export APIs from day one. Use pseudonymized IDs in analytics. Encrypt PII columns separately from non-sensitive data.

Consent management requirements under GDPR: Consent must be freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous (Article 7). Pre-ticked boxes are invalid. Granular consent per purpose (marketing vs analytics vs third-party sharing). Easy withdrawal — must be as easy to withdraw as to give. Consent records must include: who, when, what, how.

Consent Management Platform (CMP) requirements: Display a clear cookie banner before any non-essential tracking. Provide granular options (essential, analytics, marketing, third-party). Store consent proof for audit (timestamp, version, choices). Respect 'Do Not Track' signals where legally required. Re-consent after 12 months or policy change. TCF 2.2 is the IAB standard.

Cross-border data transfer mechanisms under GDPR: Standard Contractual Clauses (SCCs) are the most used mechanism since Privacy Shield invalidation (Schrems II, 2020). Adequacy decisions cover 14 countries (including UK, Japan, South Korea). Binding Corporate Rules (BCRs) for intra-group transfers. The EU-US Data Privacy Framework (2023) provides a new transatlantic transfer mechanism.

Anonymization removes all identifying information irreversibly — anonymized data is no longer personal data under GDPR. Techniques: k-anonymity (each record indistinguishable from k-1 others), l-diversity (sensitive attributes have l distinct values per group), differential privacy (add calibrated noise, epsilon parameter controls privacy/utility trade-off).

Pseudonymization replaces identifiers with tokens/aliases while maintaining a separate mapping. Unlike anonymization, pseudonymized data is still personal data under GDPR but qualifies for reduced obligations. Techniques: tokenization (random token, mapping stored separately), hashing with salt (SHA-256 + unique salt), format-preserving encryption (maintains data format for legacy systems).

Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) is mandatory under GDPR Article 35 when processing is 'likely to result in high risk' — specifically for: systematic profiling with legal effects, large-scale processing of sensitive data, and systematic monitoring of public areas. DPIA must describe processing, assess necessity, evaluate risks, and identify mitigation measures.

DPIA process: 1) Describe the data processing (what, why, how), 2) Assess necessity and proportionality, 3) Identify and assess risks to individuals (unauthorized access, data loss, discrimination), 4) Identify measures to mitigate risks (encryption, access controls, pseudonymization), 5) Document and review regularly. Consult the supervisory authority if high risk remains.

CCPA/CPRA (California) rights: Right to know (what data is collected), Right to delete, Right to opt-out of sale/sharing, Right to non-discrimination, Right to correct, Right to limit use of sensitive personal information. Applies to businesses with >$25M revenue, 100K+ consumers' data, or 50%+ revenue from selling data. Fines: $2,500 per violation, $7,500 per intentional violation.

Data Subject Access Request (DSAR) handling: Verify requester identity (2-factor verification). Search all systems (production databases, backups, logs, third-party processors, emails). Compile response within 30 days (GDPR) or 45 days (CCPA). Provide data in machine-readable format (JSON/CSV). Redact other individuals' data from the response. Free for first request; reasonable fee for repetitive requests.

HIPAA Privacy Rule protects Protected Health Information (PHI) in healthcare: names linked to health data, medical records, insurance IDs, and any of 18 identifiers combined with health information. Minimum Necessary standard limits use to what's needed. Business Associate Agreements (BAAs) required for all third parties handling PHI. Penalties: $100-50,000 per violation, up to $1.5M/year per category.

PCI DSS v4.0 requirements for payment card data: Never store full track data, CVV, or PIN after authorization. Encrypt cardholder data at rest with AES-256. Restrict access on a need-to-know basis. Maintain a vulnerability management program. Test security systems regularly. Tokenize card numbers — replace PAN with non-reversible token. Compliance levels: Level 1 (>6M transactions/year) requires annual on-site audit.

Privacy-enhancing technologies (PETs): Homomorphic encryption (compute on encrypted data without decrypting), Secure Multi-Party Computation (multiple parties compute jointly without revealing inputs), Federated Learning (train ML models without centralizing data), Zero-Knowledge Proofs (prove a statement without revealing the underlying data). Adoption growing 30-40% annually for regulated industries.

Data privacy program checklist: 1) Appoint a DPO (mandatory if public authority or large-scale processing), 2) Maintain Records of Processing Activities (ROPA), 3) Conduct DPIAs for high-risk processing, 4) Implement data breach response plan, 5) Train all employees annually (90% of breaches involve human error), 6) Review third-party processors quarterly, 7) Audit consent mechanisms every 12 months.
